data.title: Fidelity Equity-Income Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the three months ending March 31, 2025, the fund gained 3.26%, outperforming the 1.64% advance of the benchmark, the Russell 3000° Value Index.

U.S. value stocks had a mixed quarter, as post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Still, the Russell index gained this period as value stocks outpaced their growth-oriented counterparts.

On January 29, the Federal Reserve paused following three consecutive rate cuts beginning in September, extending its wait-and-see phase and indicating further reductions would depend on "real progress on inflation" or unexpected weakness in labor. The central bank affirmed this stance at its March 19 meeting, when it held steady its benchmark federal funds rate as it assessed sweeping policy changes and the effect on the economy. About a week later, new data showed that inflation climbed 2.8% in February from a year earlier- higher than the Fed's 2% target, more than economists expected and exceeding January's annual pace- as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy.

The Russell 3000* Value index rose 4.50% in the opening month of the new year, as investors reacted favorably to the definitive election outcome and potential policy changes, including the prospect of tax cuts, expansionary fiscal policy and lower regulation. Post-election momentum began to lose steam in mid-February, as the index returned 0.20% for the month. The uptrend reversed in March, as the index returned -2.93%.

Within the Russell index, the top-performing sector during Q1 was energy stocks (+8%), even as the price of oil declined. Three defensive sectors also gained: utilities (+6%), health care (+6%) and consumer staples (+5%). Communication services (+6%) also outperformed. Conversely, the typically growth-oriented information technology (-6%) and consumer discretionary (-5%) sectors declined the most.

As always, the fund was positioned with a value-oriented and defensive tilt. The fund's conservatism has historically led to outperformance during times of market volatility and moderate underperformance in "risk-on" market climates. This was largely true for its performance the past three months, as the fund outperformed overall amid the market swings this quarter.

Security selection contributed to the fund's performance versus the benchmark in Q1, with industry positioning also a positive. Stock picking in industrials lifted relative performance the most, followed by choices in health care, consumer discretionary, energy and materials. This was somewhat offset by negative stock choices in financials and utilities.

In terms of individual relative contributors, an overweight in AbbVie (ABBV)(+19%) helped the most. The stock trended higher in January after the drugmaker reported Q4 and full-year financial results that exceeded analysts' consensus expectation. Robust sales for the company's immunology therapies Skyrizi® and Rinvoq® helped the stock, in addition to management raising its sales outlook for these key drugs. Also contributing was an overweight in GE Aerospace (GE) (+20%). The stock rose in advance of the January 23 release of quarterly financial results for the maker of jet engines and aviation systems for commercial aircraft. Revenue, operating profit and earnings per share each topped analysts' consensus estimates. In addition, the firm announced an increase in share buybacks, raised its dividend by 30% and offered a financial forecast for 2025 that was consistent with lofty expectations. We continued to believe AbbVie and GE Aerospace have value, thus they were our No. 3 and No.7 holdings, respectively, on March 31. Notably, AbbVie was the largest overweight.

Conversely, avoiding index component Berkshire Hathaway (BRK.A)(BRK.B)(+17%) and holding a non-index stake in Taiwan Semiconductor (-16%) detracted from relative performance the most. In February, insurance-focused conglomerate Berkshire Hathaway reported record quarterly operating earnings for the fourth quarter of 2024, up 71% and notably higher than the consensus estimate.

Meanwhile, shares of Taiwan Semiconductor fell sharply on January 27 after a Chinese artificial intelligence startup, DeepSeek, released a free large language model that was cheaper to develop than others on the market, raising questions about massive spending on Al infrastructure and the valuations of the biggest players. Taiwan Semi was a large fund holding and No. 3 overweight at the end of March, reflecting our still-positive outlook.

Outlook and Positioning

As the second quarter begins, the global business cycle remains in expansion, with a broad shift toward monetary easing and more unsynchronized trends across major economies. In the U.S., uncertainty about policies cloud the outlook. The Fed is leaning toward additional rate cuts, but persistently high core inflation and tariffs are constraints.

The U.S. consumer has been a major driver of above-trend economic growth since the COVID-19 pandemic, supported by high growth in real wages, gains in equity market wealth and low debt service. However, economic expectations among consumers deteriorated markedly in the first quarter.

In managing the fund, we continue to use dispersion- the spread between a stock's return and the market average- as a signal for where alpha odds may be rising, then target our stock picking in those areas. Valuation dispersion has increased recently, creating, in our view, more opportunities across a range of sectors, as well as outside of the U.S.

Meanwhile, the performance of energy has been impacted by concerns over supply risk and weakening demand. Our thesis remains that the world's transition to cleaner energy sources will progress more slowly than expected and traditional energy firms will remain supply disciplined. Key fund positions in the sector are Exxon Mobil (XOM), Imperial Oil (IMO) and Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ).

Lastly, with lower-end consumers under increasing pressure due to the lasting effects of inflation, we've invested the fund in stocks of consumer discretionary companies that have a valuation that reflects consumer weakness. Key positions here as of March 31 include TJX Companies (TJX) and McDonalds (MCD).

Looking ahead, we will continue to use spread dispersion as a signal for where alpha odds may be rising. We plan to continue focusing on large price/value disconnects in quality companies to achieve our three main investment goals: investment return, minimizing downside capture, and yield income.

This quarter we did not make any major changes to the fund's positioning. The largest sector overweight at quarter end was consumer staples, where we favor high-quality companies that we think can pass along rising costs. In addition, stocks in this category typically perform well in a recession. Here, some of the fund's key positions include big box retailers Walmart (WMT) and Target (TGT). We also hold stakes in consumer product giant Procter & Gamble and Alimentation Couche-Tard (OTCPK:ANCTF), a Canadian operator of convenience stores with an attractive valuation and underestimated cash-flow potential. Notably, Walmart and P&G were the fund's No. 6 and No.8 holdings, respectively, at the end of the first quarter.

In communications services- another sector overweight- the fund's largest positions were media and entertainment giant Walt Disney (DIS), cable stalwart Comcast (CMCSA) and telecom company T-Mobile.

In contrast, the fund was underweight real estate, financials and industrials. Financials, however, was the fund's biggest absolute weight at quarter end, making up 21% of assets. Here, notable positions included JPMorgan Chase, Wells Fargo (WFC) and Bank of America (BAC), the fund's No. 1, No. 9, and No.10 holdings, respectively, at quarter end. JPMorgan and Bank of America are market-share gainers executing well at reasonable valuations, while Wells Fargo is a turnaround story. We also favor strong regional banks, including M&T Bank (MTB) and Huntington Bancshares (HBAN).

data.title: Fidelity Dividend Growth Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Performance Review

For the first quarter of 2025, the fund's Retail Class shares returned-5.29%, trailing the-4.27% result of the benchmark S&P 500 ((SP500), (SPX)) index.

U.S. stocks returned-4.27% in the first three months of the year,according to the S&P 500° index, as post-election optimism gave way to investor concern about the impact of a flurry of executive actions by the new administration, disruption to government programs and a spike in uncertainty stemming from shifting policy priorities, especially related to trade and tariffs. Amid rising volatility, the index gained 4.64% through February 19, but then sharply reversed course and returned-8.51% through the end of the quarter, particularly dragged down by growth stocks and smaller-caps.

In the first quarter, the Federal Reserve held its key overnight lending rate steady following three cuts in the final four months of 2024, as it assessed sweeping policy changes and their effect on the economy. New data showed that inflation climbed 2.8% in February from a year earlier- higher than the Fed's 2% target, more than economists expected and exceeding January's annual pace- as underlying price pressure intensified. Meanwhile, confidence and sentiment among U.S. consumers continued to slip, mostly reflecting a gloomier outlook for inflation and the economy.

For the quarter, growth shares (-8%) within the S&P 500° widely lagged value (0%). The index's decline was narrowly concentrated in the growth-oriented consumer discretionary (-14%) and information technology (-13%) sectors, as well as communication services (-6%).These three groups combined represented about half of the index for the three months. In contrast, energy stocks rose 10% to lead the way, even as the price of oil declined. Four defensive sectors also gained: health care (+7%), consumer staples (+5%), utilities (+5%), and real estate (+4%). Financials advanced roughly 4%, boosted by expectations for easing regulation within the sector, while materials gained 3% and industrials was roughly flat.

Versus the benchmark, stock selection in information technology notably detracted from performance, although an underweight exposure partly offset that impact. Investment choices in financials also hurt, along with an underweight in health care.

At the stock level, out-of-benchmark exposure to Marvell Technology topped our list of relative detractors. The stock returned -43% for the past three months. In early March, the provider of data infrastructure and semiconductor solutions reported slightly better-than-expected fiscal Q4 earnings. However, in an environment of high valuations and elevated expectations for artificial intelligence-related solutions providers, analysts said that the company's quarterly datacenter business results- which were in line with consensus views- likely proved somewhat disappointing and contributed to the stock's decline. That said, much of the downward pressure on shares came earlier in the year, when a Chinese generative Al startup, DeepSeek, released a free large language model that was cheaper to develop, raising questions about spending on Al infrastructure and the valuations of the biggest players, including chipmakers.

An outsized stake in Alphabet (GOOG) GOOGL) detracted as well. The shares returned-18% for the quarter, falling beginning in early February, as the advertising giant and parent of the Google search engine announced quarterly revenue that reflected a slowdown in sales at its cloud-computing and device businesses. Investors were hoping strength in cloud computing would ease the impact of recent weakness in the company's core advertising business. Meanwhile, management said it is meaningfully accelerating investments in data centers that power artificial intelligence. Alphabet also faces regulatory pressure, including a recently opened antitrust probe from the Chinese government.

Conversely, positioning in the consumer discretionary sector was the primary bright spot among sectors, along with a large overweight in energy. Among individual holdings, avoiding benchmark components Tesla (TSLA) (-36%) and Amazon (AMZN) (-13%) notably contributed to relative performance. Neither company pays a dividend, so these stocks were not a good fit with the fund's income-oriented mandate.

Outlook and Positioning

The almost giddy mood investors were in at the end of 2024 soon faded away amid the onslaught of directives from the new administration. As the market environment cooled, we've somewhat tempered our enthusiasm for the artificial intelligence space in the short term, as cyclical weakness might trump secular bullishness for the time being. However, our view remains that generative Al is a transformative long-term trend.

As of quarter end, we favor industrial companies to play this trend,rather than the technology names that have been so popular with investors lately. For example, GE Vernova (GEV) is a meaningful overweight at quarter end that we added to during the period. The company is a global leader in the electric power industry. Its largest division makes gas turbines, which are used to generate electricity.Amid the broader global push toward electrification and decarbonization- including supplying the world's increased power needs due to the advent of generative Al- Vernova is very well positioned. In addition to its primary business manufacturing gas turbines, the firm has a rapidly growing portfolio of solutions in wind, nuclear and hydro power and storage.

We also like the commercial aerospace area of industrials, a theme that is mainly reflected in the capital goods segment. Supply chains in the group have eased and demand for widebody jets has picked up, reflecting a global recovery in air traffic. A key holding here is Boeing (BA), the fund's No.1 overweight. Happily, a seven-week strike at the company was resolved in November, and production is getting back to normal. What's more, in our conversations with Boeing's airline customers, we've heard favorable comments about improved quality and on-time deliveries. Additionally, the firm raised roughly $24 billion of capital in the fall by selling additional shares of stock, helping to strengthen its balance sheet. All told, we're anticipating better times for Boeing.

The energy sector also remains a significant overweight. The rationale here is low valuations, corporate policies about returning capital to shareholders through dividends and stock buybacks, and the steady albeit slow growth in global energy consumption. In this sector, the fund is focused on mission-critical infrastructure providers and exploration & production firms with low decline rates and predictable capital spending policies. Individual energy positions of note at quarter end were Energy Transfer (ET) and Enterprise Products Partners (EPD).

After reducing the portfolio's allocation to semiconductors, information technology was our largest sector underweight as of March 31. The fund had already been underweight the tech sector coming into the quarter, mainly due to a relatively light stake in Apple, which is part of the technology hardware, storage, and peripherals group. Reducing the overweight in chip stocks further increased the technology underweight.

Consumer discretionary was another significant sector underweight at quarter end. In line with our broadly cautious outlook about some parts of the economy, we favored companies in other sectors over those in the discretionary sector. Notable benchmark components in this latter group not held in the fund were Amazon.com, Tesla and Home Depot (HD).

Overall, top individual overweights at the end of March included Boeing, Wells Fargo (WFC) and GE Vernova. Top non-benchmark positions were Brookfield, Energy Transfer and Allison Transmission Holdings (ALSN). The top-three underweights were Apple, Amazon.com and Berkshire Hathaway.

data.title: Fidelity Blue Chip Growth ETF Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Fidelity Blue Chip Growth ETF (BATS:FBCG) Performance Review

For the quarter, the exchange-traded fund's net asset value gained 20.28% and its market price advanced 20.69%, topping the 17.84%result of the benchmark, the Russell 1000* Growth Index.

U.S. large-cap growth stocks ended June at an all-time high after erasing a brief but steep early-April decline that followed President Trump's announcement of a sweeping tariff plan, resulting in rising volatility and concern about a global trade war. The benchmark returned-12.76% from April 2 through April 8, but then sharply reversed course and rose 32.76% through midyear.

The historically fast rebound was set off by a 90-day pause on most planned tariffs, and sustained by robust corporate profits, a solid economy and inflation trending around the Federal Reserve's 2%target. These tailwinds overshadowed rising tension in the Middle East and lingering uncertainty about trade ahead of the July 8 expiration of the tariff pause.

The Fed held steady its benchmark federal funds rate in May and June, as expected, stating it is open to cutting rates in the second half of the year but prefers a wait-and-see stance for the time being,given its expectation for meaningful inflation in the coming months,as well as uncertainty about the impact of tariffs on prices. The central bank cut its projection for U.S. economic growth this year and raised its outlook for inflation and unemployment.

The fund's bias for high-growth, cyclical stocks contributed to performance versus the benchmark in Q2, as economically sensitive and growth-oriented assets outperformed. As a result, stock picking in information technology helped most, especially among software& services holdings. Positioning among semiconductor stocks also contributed, mostly due to overweights in Al-focused chipmakers.

The top individual relative contributor was an overweight in Nvidia(+46%), the top holding by a wide margin and second-largest overweight on June 30. On May 28, the dominant maker of advanced graphics chips for generative Al systems reported sharp increases in sales and profit for the three months ending April 27.Revenue surged 69% to a new quarterly record, even though the company was effectively shut out of China, one of the largest markets for its products. Nvidia's data-center business grew even faster, rising 73% due to strong demand for chips and other components used in Al computing systems.

Another notable relative contributor was a non-benchmark stake in CoreWeave, which specializes in providing cloud-based graphics processing unit infrastructure to Al developers. The position gained 271% the past three months, which roughly matched the period since its March 28 initial public offering. In mid-May, the company reported better-than-expected quarterly revenue, and management guided second-quarter sales higher than Wall Street's consensus expectation, reflecting strong growth driven by increased adoption of Al by enterprises.

Conversely, underweights in chipmaker Broadcom (AVGO)(+65%) and software and cloud giant Microsoft(+33%) were the largest individual detractors versus the benchmark. Both stocks benefited from better-than-expected Q1 financial results, with Broadcom driven by its custom chips and software that speed up Al computations and Microsoft by its Azure cloud-computing services.

We added to both Broadcom and Microsoft the past three months,as we became more optimistic about their growth prospects. In particular, we believe Microsoft is well-positioned to become a leader in generative Al with its Copilot offering for Office 365. Still,Microsoft was the largest underweight at midyear, while Broadcom was No.4. Both are among the fund's top holdings, given their big representation in the benchmark.

Outlook and Positioning

Volatility eased in the second quarter, as the Trump administration backed off from the immediate implementation and high tariffs announced on the President's April 2"Liberation Day."

As the market gained more certainty about the path of trade policy,equity prices erased the steep downturn from mid-February to early April, with the benchmark Russell 1000® Growth Index reaching midyear at a new high.

In the U.S., where two-thirds of gross domestic product is driven by consumer spending, consumer health is still strong relative to history, despite recent concerns regarding the impact of tariffs.

The savings rate is historically high (despite coming down from inflated levels during the pandemic), domestic demand for travel appears to be resilient and consumer sentiment improved since declining sharply in March and April at the height of tariff-induced uncertainty. Also, gas prices are reasonable, despite recent geopolitical upheaval in the Middle East.

On the valuation front, at midyear the market is trading at a level that slightly exceeds fair value, in our opinion, but there remain pockets where recession fears and/or cyclical factors are contributing to attractive share prices.

On June 30, the fund has exposure to some of these cyclical segments of the market, such as semiconductors and consumer discretionary, where we believe earnings estimates have largely already been reset. As the second half of 2025 begins, the portfolio remains offensively positioned, emphasizing businesses with durable earning growth that the market underappreciates.

Artificial intelligence remains an important secular growth trend and driver of market returns, despite macro uncertainty. We remain confident in this trend long term, and we have started to see improved returns on investment for cloud-service providers that have fueled much of the spending on Al. We will continue to scour the market for companies using Al in innovative ways to drive earnings growth.

As of midyear, the fund has notable overweights in semiconductor makers Nvidia and Marvell Technology. Nvidia is the leading supplier of systems used for generative Al models. Marvell enables several cloud players to develop internal chips for generative Al. We believe we are still at the early stages of generative Al adoption and the next several years will bring to market many new, innovative business models and use cases. Nvidia remains the fund's largest holding, while Marvell is a top-10 holding and a significant overweight on June 30, even though we modestly reduced it in Q2.

Also in the tech, cloud computing is enabling the digital transformation of businesses across all verticals. The fund continues to have exposure to the software firms and select semiconductor companies poised to benefit over time.

The fund's largest sector overweights as of June 30 are consumer discretionary and communication services. In the former, the portfolio has exposure to companies that we believe are leaders in e-commerce, including a large position in Amazon (AMZN).The fund also holds positions to capitalize on athleisure and sneaker trends, as consumers around the globe increasingly prioritize health, fitness and adoption of more athleisure wear. Furthermore, a growing trend toward casualization, including the adoption of sneakers for work, supports these companies.

Investment Approach

● Fidelity Blue Chip Growth ETF is a domestic equity growth strategy with a large-cap bias.

● Our investment approach focuses on companies we believe have above-average earnings-growth potential with a sustainable business model, for which the market has mispriced the rate and/or durability of growth.

● In particular, we look for events that might provide a business catalyst- such as product cycles, a change in management and turnaround situations- that could add to a stock's true value. We believe choosing companies with a competitive advantage, pricing power and a strong management team will deliver superior earnings in the long term.

•We look to exploit inappropriate valuations in the market through bottom-up, fundamental analysis, working in concert with Fidelity's global research team.

CHARACTERISTICS

Earnings-Per-Share Growth Trailing measures the growth in reported earnings per share over trailing one- and five-year periods.

Median Market Cap identifies the median market capitalization of the portfolio or benchmark as determined by the underlying security market caps.

Price-to-Book(P/B) Ratio is the ratio of a company's current share price to reported accumulated profits and capital.

Price/Cash Flow is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months cash flow per share.

Price-to-Earnings(P/E) Ratio(IBES 1-Year Forecast) is the ratio of a company's current share price to Wall Street analysts' estimates of earnings.

Price-to-Earnings(P/E) Ratio Trailing is the ratio of a company's current share price to its trailing 12-months earnings per share.

Return on Equity(ROE) 5-Year Trailing is the ratio of a company's last five years historical profitability to its shareholders' equity.Preferred stock is included as part of each company's net worth.

Sales-Per-Share Growth measures the growth in reported sales over the specified past time period.

Weighted Average Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the average equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

Weighted Median Market Cap identifies the market capitalization of the median equity holding as determined by the dollars invested in the portfolio or benchmark.

FUND RISKS

The objective of the actively managed ETF Tracking Basket is to construct a portfolio of stocks and representative index ETFs that tracks the daily performance of an actively managed ETF without

exposing current holdings,trading activities, or internal equity research.The Tracking Basket is designed to conceal any nonpublic information about the underlying portfolio and only uses the Fund's latest publicly disclosed holdings, representative ETFs, and the publicly known daily performance in its construction. Although the Tracking Basket is intended to provide investors with enough information to allow for an effective arbitrage mechanism that will keep the market price of the Fund at or close to the underlying NAV per share of the Fund, there is a risk(which may increase during periods of market disruption or volatility) that market prices will vary significantly from the underlying NAV of the Fund;ETFs trading on the basis of a published Tracking Basket may trade at a wider bid/ask spread than ETFs that publish their portfolios on a daily basis, especially during periods of market disruption or volatility, and, therefore, may cost investors more to trade, and although the Fund seeks to benefit from keeping its portfolio information secret, market participants may attempt to use the Tracking Basket to identify a Fund's trading strategy, which, if successful, could result in such market participants engaging in certain predatory trading practices that may have the potential to harm the Fund and its shareholders. Because shares are traded in the secondary market, a broker may charge a commission to execute a transaction in shares, and an investor may incur the cost of the spread between the price at which a dealer will buy shares and the price at which a dealer will sell shares. The fund may have a limited number of financial institutions that act as authorized participants, none of which are obligated to engage in creation and/ or redemption transactions. To the extent that those authorized participants do not engage in creation and redemption orders, there may be a significantly diminished trading market for fund shares or fund shares may trade at a discount(or premium) to NAV and possibly face trading halts and/or de-listing. Unlike mutual funds,ETF shares are bought and sold at market price,which may be higher or lower than their NAV, and are not individually redeemed from the fund. Stock markets, especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer,political,regulatory,market,or economic developments. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate, currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks."Growth" stocks can react differently to issuer, political, market,and economic developments than the market as a whole and other types of stocks. The fund may have additional volatility because it can invest a significant portion of assets in securities of a small number of individual issuers.

TRADING INFORMATION

Bid/Ask Spread

In the case of exchange-traded securities, market liquidity is managed by the market maker community. Market makers provide prices at which they will buy(the bid) and sell(the ask) securities.The difference between the bid and ask prices is the bid/ask spread. The bid/ask spread gives an investor a sense of the liquidity and the transaction costs associated with a security.Securities with wide bid/ask spreads tend to be less liquid and

have higher cost of ownership. Those with tight bid/ask spreads tend to be more liquid and have a lower cost of ownership.

Premium/Discount

The exchange-quoted price of an ETF frequently differs from the ETF's underlying value. The difference in these values represents an ETF's discount or premium. In the case of a discount, the exchange-quoted share price is lower than the ETF's underlying value. An ETF is at a premium when the exchange-quoted price is greater than its underlying value. An ETF's discount or premium fluctuates over time and is influenced by such factors as the transaction costs of the underlying holdings, the cost of creation and redemption of shares, and the average daily volume.The magnitude and volatility of an ETF's premium/discount will affect an investor's holding period return on an ETF. If an investor buys shares at a discount and sells at a premium, they should outperform the NAV return of the ETF. On the other hand, if an investor buys at a premium and sells at a discount, they should underperform the NAV return of the ETF.

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater(less) than 1 is more(less) volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return(fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index.R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation,to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance.It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns(the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month"risk free"return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns.The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk.The three month"risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.

Tracking Error is the divergence between the price behavior of a position or a portfolio and the price behavior of a benchmark,creating an unexpected profit or loss.

data.title: Fidelity New Markets Income Fund Q2 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Emerging-Markets Bond Market Review

Emerging-markets debt gained 3.32% in the second quarter, as measured by the J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index Global Diversified Index, rising amid yield spread tightening and investors improved appetite for risk. Within the index, high-yield sovereign bonds outpaced their investment-grade counterparts, as investors looked outside the U.S. for yield and investment opportunities.

Geopolitical tension and concerns about the impact of foreign policy and tariffs by the Trump administration continued. On April 2, President Trump announced a baseline 10% tariff on all countries, with additional measures on European countries and China.Although a 90-day pause on most planned tariffs was swiftly implemented, concerns about reciprocal tariffs spurred a risk-off trade through April 8.

Despite a broad sell-off in global bonds and U.S. Treasurys in May, following Moody's (MCO) downgrade of the U.S. credit rating, emerging-markets debt gained 1.12% for the month. The index advanced 2.41% in June, amid tension in the Middle East and uncertainty about trade ahead of the July 8 expiration of the tariff pause.

The Fed held steady its benchmark federal funds rate in May and June, as expected, stating it is open to cutting rates in the second half of the year. Outside the U.S., monetary easing provided a tailwind, as many central banks continued to cut interest rates, including the eurozone. China has seen improvement through a pickup in industrial activity and regulatory policy easing. Against this backdrop, performance among the index's 70 country constituents was largely positive, with all but three delivering a gain.Ecuador (+48%) led by the widest margin.The bonds rallied in April after incumbent President Daniel Noboa was reelected. In June, the International Monetary Fund reached an agreement on the secondary review, raising Ecuador's funding to $5 billion.

Lebanon(+22%), the Maldives (+14%), Ghana (+100%) and Zambia (+100%), each a small component of the index in Q2, also notably outperformed amid the high-yield rally. Lebanese bonds climbed early in the quarter on hopes for an economic recovery following parliament's election of army chief Joseph Aoun as president in January, along with the potential for banking reform.

Argentina(+9%) was another strong-performing bond market for the quarter.The IMF approved a $20 billion loan agreement with Argentina to support the country's economic stabilization plan, boost its external position and foster sustainable growth.

Conversely, Senegal (-9%), Venezuela (-7%), and Ukraine (-4%) were the only components to decline in the second quarter. In June, Senegal's finance ministry released data showing the country's debt surged to 119% of its gross domestic product last year, which was higher than analysts expected. Meanwhile, Venezuela bonds were held back by concern about possible U.S. tariffs and sanctions.Senegal and Venezuela were small index weightings in Q2.

Lastly, Ukrainian bonds were hampered by dwindling hopes for a peace deal amid the country's prolonged conflict with Russia. In addition, in June the finance ministry sparked concerns the country could default on its debt when it said it had yet to strike a deal with creditors after several weeks negotiating the restructuring of Ukraine's $20 billion debt.

Performance Review

For the quarter, the fund's Retail Class shares gained 2.47%, lagging the 3.32% advance of the benchmark, the J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index Global Diversified Index.

In Q2, we continued to focus on sovereign and quasi-sovereign debt denominated in U.S. dollars, and, to a lesser extent, on local-currency debt and emerging-markets corporate securities.

In managing the fund, we seek to consistently outperform the fund's benchmark, which is denominated in U.S. dollars.

Exposure to corporate bonds detracted from the fund's performance versus the benchmark the past three months, more than offsetting the relative contribution of an allocation to U.S. dollar-denominated sovereign bonds.

By region, exposure to Latin America hurt most by far. This included security selection in Brazil, particularly the fund's holdings in corporate bonds. An overweight in Venezuela also detracted, given this bond market's underperformance. Venezuelan bonds returned to the benchmark last year, after having been removed from it in 2019. The fund's holdings in Venezuela securities returned -6.49% for the second quarter.

Security selection among corporate bonds issued in Ghana was another negative, as was a non-benchmark stake in U.S. Treasurys.We use Treasurys from time to time as a risk-management tool and a means to increase the fund's duration, a measure of sensitivity to interest rates. The fund's U.S. Treasury exposure rose modestly in lieu of fewer opportunities among EM sovereigns given the tightening in yield spreads.

Conversely, an overweight in Ecuador- the strongest-performing country component in the index the past three months- contributed most to relative performance. It also helped to underweight China and Bahrain, two bond markets that underperformed for the three months.

We'll also mention positioning in Mexico as a notable contributor.Here, an overweight in bonds of state oil company Pemex were a bright spot.

Outlook and Positioning

As we look ahead to the second half of 2025, our outlook ischallenged by economic policy uncertainty and incipient signs of a U.S. economic slowdown. Trade policy and the tariff regime are front and center, based on their direct impact on various emerging-markets issuers, most notably China. As important are the knock-on effects, including the outlook for U.S. monetary policy, the level and shape of the U.S. yield curve, the value of the U.S. dollar, and the trajectory of global economic growth.

Globally, geopolitical risk remains elevated as of midyear. We are closely watching the Russia-Ukraine and Israel-Hamas wars, U.S.-China relations, and economic uncertainty that is present in a number of emerging markets.

The tight U.S. labor market has prompted the U.S. Federal Reserve to hold steady its monetary policy, but now trade policy may complicate the forward path of inflation and the trajectory of U.S. growth. The most likely scenario, in our view, is one that embeds a softening of the current U.S. stance towards most trading partners, with a narrower focus primarily on China.

At the end of June, the fund is positioned to generate alpha if our outlook proves correct, but also to mitigate downside capture if we are wrong. This is part of our longstanding strategy to tighten the distribution of portfolio alpha. As always, our fundamental research remains invaluable in our effort, and we will continue to lean into this resource to build conviction during these uncertain times.

We'll note that over the course of the quarter, we added exposure to Uzbekistan and Turkey. We also upped the fund's stake in U.S.Treasuries to 5.60% at midyear, an increase of 70 bps since the end of March.

Meanwhile, the biggest decreases by country were to United Arab Emirates, Oman, and Qatar due to their tighter yield spreads and less attractive relative value.

Overall, we increased the fund's exposure to non-U.S.-dollar denominated debt and decreased our holdings in corporate bonds

As of June 30, the top country overweights in the fund were Mexico, Venezuela, Brazil and Colombia, whereas underweights as of midyear were most pronounced in China, the Philippines, Bahrain, Poland, and Malaysia.

FUND INFORMATION

Manager(s): Timothy Gill, Nader Nazmi

Trading Symbol: FNMIX

Start Date: May 03, 1993

Size(in millions): $4,942.77

Morningstar Category: Fund Emerging Markets Bond

Fixed income investments entail interest rate risk(as interest rates rise bond prices usually fall), the risk of issuer default, issuer credit risk and inflation risk.Stock markets,especially foreign markets, are volatile and can decline significantly in response to adverse issuer, political, regulatory, market, or economic developments. Foreign securities are subject to interest rate,currency exchange rate, economic, and political risks, all of which are magnified in emerging markets. Lower-quality bonds can be more volatile and have greater risk of default than higher-quality bonds. The fund may have additional volatility because it can invest a significant portion of assets in securities of a small number of individual issuers.

Investment Approach

● Fidelity New Markets Income Fund is an emerging-markets (EM) bond strategy that offers dedicated exposure to sovereign debt of EM nations around the world.

● The fund focuses primarily on U.S.-dollar-denominated sovereign debt, and to a lesser extent on EM corporate bonds.

● Our investment approach in managing the fund begins with country research, rooted in macroeconomic analysis, to help identify undervalued sovereign bonds with positive catalysts.

● We incorporate global macro perspectives to supplement our country-level research. Our extensive experience investing in emerging markets and deep research capabilities support us in our efforts to take advantage of long-term trends and short-term opportunities.

Duration differs from maturity in that it considers a security's interest payments in addition to the amount of time until the security reaches maturity, and also takes into account certain maturity shortening features(e.g., demand features, interest rate resets, and call options) when applicable. Securities with longer durations generally tend to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than securities with shorter durations. A fund with a longer average duration generally can be expected to be more sensitive to interest rate changes than a fund with a shorter average duration.

30-day SEC Yield is a standard yield calculation developed by the Securities and Exchange Commission for bond funds. The yield is calculated by dividing the net investment income per share earned during the 30-day period by the maximum offering price per share on the last day of the period. The yield figure reflects the dividends and interest earned during the 30-day period, after the deduction of the fund's expenses. It is sometimes referred to as"SEC 30-Day Yield" or"standardized yield".

30-Day SEC Restated Yield is the fund's 30-day yield without applicable waivers or reimbursements, stated as of month-end.

Net Asset Value is the dollar value of one share of a fund;determined by taking the total assets of a fund, subtracting the total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding.

% Rank in Morningstar Category is the fund's total-return percentile rank relative to all funds that have the same Morningstar Category.The highest(or most favorable) percentile rank is 1 and the lowest(or least favorable) percentile rank is 100. The top-performing fund in a category will always receive a rank of 1%.%Rank in Morningstar Category is based on total returns which include reinvested dividends and capital gains, if any, and exclude sales charges. Multiple share classes of a fund have a common portfolio but impose different expense structures.

RELATIVE WEIGHTS

Relative weights represents the% of fund assets in a particular market segment, asset class or credit quality relative to the benchmark. A positive number represents an overweight, and a negative number is an underweight. The fund's benchmark is listed immediately under the fund name in the Performance Summary.

3-YEAR RISK/RETURN STATISTICS

Beta is a measure of the volatility of a fund relative to its benchmark index. A beta greater(less) than 1 is more(less)volatile than the index.

Information Ratio measures a fund's active return(fund's average monthly return minus the benchmark's average monthly return) in relation to the volatility of its active returns.

R-Squared measures how a fund's performance correlates with a benchmark index's performance and shows what portion of it can be explained by the performance of the overall market/index.R-Squared ranges from 0, meaning no correlation, to 1, meaning perfect correlation. An R-Squared value of less than 0.5 indicates that annualized alpha and beta are not reliable performance statistics.

Sharpe Ratio is a measure of historical risk-adjusted performance.It is calculated by dividing the fund's excess returns(the fund's average annual return for the period minus the 3-month"risk free" return rate) and dividing it by the standard deviation of the fund's returns. The higher the ratio, the better the fund's return per unit of risk.The three month"risk free" rate used is the 90-day Treasury Bill rate.

Standard Deviation is a statistical measurement of the dispersion of a fund's return over a specified time period. Fidelity calculates standard deviations by comparing a fund's monthly returns to its average monthly return over a 36-month period, and then annualizes the number. Investors may examine historical standard deviation in conjunction with historical returns to decide whether a fund's volatility would have been acceptable given the returns it would have produced. A higher standard deviation indicates a wider dispersion of past returns and thus greater historical volatility. Standard deviation does not indicate how the fund actually performed, but merely indicates the volatility of its returns over time.
